title: Pull cut
subtitle: The knife cut that draws toward the body, used sparingly and only braced against the chest
excerpt: The pull cut draws the knife blade toward the body across the workpiece. Used sparingly and only in the chest-brace position, it reaches areas a push cut cannot: the inside of a curved handle, the underside of a bowl neck, the concave face of a hollowed treen piece. This technique covers the grip, the chest-brace requirement, when to use it, and the three common mistakes that cause accidents.
sourceNotes: Early-twentieth-century Swedish slöjd manuals for the pull-cut safety protocol. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the chest-brace carving position.

content.text: The
pull cut

is the most controlled cut available to a carver: it uses forearm and wrist strength with the workpiece braced firmly against the chest, which limits the blade-arc to a short, predictable path. Its controlled nature makes it right for fine details that a
push cut
cannot reach. It is used sparingly, not as a default; the push cut handles the majority of shaping work.
What it does

The blade travels across the workpiece toward the body, slicing rather than pushing. The forearm does the work; the shoulder stays still. Because the body absorbs the resistance, the cut is very controllable and applies well on tight inside curves, the underside of a spoon neck, or the concave face of a bowl edge where a push cut would require an awkward angle.
When to use it

Use the pull cut when the push cut and the thumb-pivot cut cannot reach the surface. Three common cases. One, inside a curved handle, where a push cut from either end would run against the grain. Two, cleaning up the joint between spoon neck and handle base. Three, taking a thin shaving from a hollow area, where a push-cut blade would sit too flat to bite into the wood.
What you need

A sloyd knife that is sharp: a dull blade under pull-cut pressure will drag and slip unpredictably. The workpiece must be held firmly; it cannot be held loosely and controlled simultaneously. The strop should be immediately to hand.
Step-by-step
Step 1: Position the chest brace

Sit upright. Hold the workpiece in the non-dominant hand with the carving face pointing away from the body. Press the elbow of the knife arm against the side of the chest. This brace limits the blade-arc: even if the knife slips, it can travel only as far as the extended forearm reaches before the elbow locks against the chest. The blade-arc must end in open air before it can reach any body part.
Step 2: Set the grip

Hold the knife in a closed fist with the thumb on the spine of the handle. The thumb does not push the blade; it controls the depth of cut by how much wrist flex is applied. The non-dominant hand holds the workpiece with the fingers wrapped around the far side; the thumb of the non-dominant hand may rest lightly on the near face of the workpiece to gauge depth but never extends into the path of the blade.
Step 3: Execute the cut

Place the blade on the wood surface at the start of the cut. Draw the forearm back in a smooth arc, pulling the blade across the wood. The cut should peel a thin, even shaving. Stop the cut before the blade clears the end of the workpiece into air; the final few millimetres of each pull cut are the point at which inexperienced carvers let the blade continue past the wood and into the thumb or palm of the holding hand.
Step 4: Rotate the workpiece for the next pass

Rotate the piece between pull cuts so each subsequent pass covers a slightly different face of the wood. The cut accumulates gradually; a series of light, well-placed pull cuts shapes a tight concave area more precisely than a single heavy cut.
What it looks like when it is right

A clean pull cut peels a thin, slightly translucent shaving that curls away from the blade and falls. The cut surface is smooth without torn fibres. The depth of cut is consistent across the full length of the stroke; the blade does not wander deeper or shallower.
Common mistakes
Letting the cut continue past the end of the workpiece: the blade clears the wood and the momentum carries it into the holding hand. Stop each cut while the blade is still on wood.
Holding the workpiece too loosely: the piece shifts under the pull and the cut goes deeper than intended. Grip the work firmly and lock the elbow against the chest before starting.
Using the pull cut as a default: most shaping work is done more safely with a push cut. Reach for the pull cut only when push-cut geometry does not work.
